name: Bald Hill
short_description: Bald Hill near Roxbury offers easy rockhounding for quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens in the New England Upland region. This moderate-elevation site is ideal for collectors seeking pegmatite and...
description:
Bald Hill stands at 1263 feet elevation in the Cheshire County highlands, placing it within New Hampshire's metamorphic bedrock terrain. The area sits in the heart of the New England Upland province, where Precambrian and Paleozoic metamorphic rocks dominate the landscape. Rockhounders visit this location to collect quartz crystals, feldspar specimens, and mica sheets exposed in the moderate slopes surrounding the hilltop.

The geological foundation here reflects intense mountain-building activity during the Acadian orogeny (roughly 400 million years ago), when collision between continental plates transformed existing sedimentary and igneous rocks into metamorphic gneisses and schists. These metamorphic rocks frequently weather to expose fresh pegmatitic zones rich in large feldspar crystals and quartz veins. The moderate slope and accessible terrain make this an excellent location for both beginners and experienced collectors.

Visitors should expect mixed forest cover on the lower slopes with more open terrain near the summit. The rocky footing typical of New Hampshire hilltop collecting sites provides good mineral exposure without requiring technical climbing skills. Spring melt and seasonal rainfall here create natural erosion that continuously exposes new mineral specimens.

Collectors typically work the exposed bedrock outcrops and talus slopes where gravity and water have naturally concentrated heavier mineral fragments. The moderate elevation keeps this site accessible year-round with reasonable weather windows, making it one of the more reliable rockhounding destinations in the region.
history: Bald Hill sits within the New England Upland's metamorphic bedrock province, shaped by intense Acadian mountain-building activity approximately 400 million years ago. The pegmatitic rocks and quartz veins exposed here formed as residual fluids from cooling granitic bodies crystallized within metamorphic host rocks, creating the feldspar and quartz specimens collectors seek today.
tips: Search the rocky outcrops and talus slopes on the south and east-facing sides of the hill where weathering has exposed fresh pegmatitic zones with large feldspar crystals. Bring a basic rock hammer and chisel for carefully separating specimens from the outcrop without damaging them. Look for milky white and translucent quartz veins cross-cutting the darker metamorphic host rock, and examine feldspar crystals for the characteristic pink or white coloration of feldspars in New England pegmatites. The moderate slope allows productive collecting within a 30-minute walking radius of a reasonable parking area.
terrain_notes: Bald Hill rises to 1263 feet with moderate slopes dominated by mixed deciduous and coniferous forest. Rocky footing is typical, with exposed bedrock outcrops and talus fields scattered across the hillside. Walking distance from parking to productive collecting areas is approximately 0.25 to 0.5 miles, with uneven terrain requiring sturdy boots but no technical scrambling.
best_season: May - October. Spring through early fall offers the best combination of accessibility and weather for hiking the moderate slopes. Summer months (June-August) provide the longest daylight but can be hot during midday hours. Fall colors peak in late September through mid-October, and trails are generally dry through October. Winter ice and snow make the rocky terrain hazardous.
written_directions: From Roxbury center, take NH Route 119 east for approximately 2 miles. Look for a small pullout or parking area on the north side of the road near mile marker 10, where private or town-maintained land access may be available. Contact the Roxbury town office to confirm current access and parking. GPS coordinates point to the hilltop location; verify access before driving toward the summit, as portions may cross private property.
road_conditions: Paved highway (NH Route 119) to parking area; final approach may be unpaved or walking trail only
parking_notes: Small pullout or informal parking area on NH Route 119; capacity appears limited to 2-3 vehicles. Confirm access with Roxbury town office before visiting.
collection_rules: Verify current access and collection permissions with Roxbury town offices and Cheshire County before collecting. Personal use only. No commercial collection. Respect private property boundaries and posted signage. Pack out all excess waste.
rules:

Personal use only

nearest_services: Roxbury village center approximately 2 miles west; limited services. Keene, NH (approximately 15 miles south) offers full services including gas, food, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Relatively easy terrain accessible to most hikers. Moderate slopes with rocky footing but no steep scrambling required. Uneven ground and exposed roots necessitate sturdy hiking boots. Walking distance to prime collecting areas is less than 0.5 miles from parking, making this suitable for families with school-age children and people with basic fitness levels.

faq.question: What gemstones and minerals can I find at Bald Hill near Roxbury, New Hampshire?
faq.answer: Bald Hill is known for quartz crystals, feldspar specimens, mica sheets, and garnet fragments. The site's metamorphic bedrock frequently contains pegmatitic zones with large, well-formed feldspar crystals in white and pink varieties. Milky quartz veins are common and easily spotted against the darker host rock.

faq.question: Is Bald Hill rockhounding site free to access, or are there fees or permits required?
faq.answer: Bald Hill does not charge collection fees, but access permission should be verified with the Roxbury town office before visiting. The site is not formally designated public land, so confirming current access is essential. Once access is confirmed, rockhounding is free for personal use.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Bald Hill in New Hampshire?
faq.answer: May through October is ideal, with peak conditions during late June through September when trails are dry and weather is mild. Fall (September-October) offers excellent conditions and beautiful scenery. Winter ice and snow make the rocky terrain hazardous, so plan your visit during warmer months.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Bald Hill, and is it suitable for beginners and children?
faq.answer: Bald Hill is an easy to moderate rockhounding location suitable for beginners and children ages 6 and older. The moderate slopes and short walking distances (under 0.5 miles from parking) require only basic fitness. Rocky footing and loose material are present but no technical climbing or scrambling is necessary.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need to rockhound at Bald Hill near Roxbury?
faq.answer: Bring a basic rock hammer, chisel, and safety glasses for specimen extraction. Sturdy hiking boots are essential due to rocky terrain and loose stones. A backpack for specimens, water bottle, and a field guide to New England minerals will enhance your visit. A small magnifying glass helps identify feldspar varieties and mica layers.

faq.question: Are dogs allowed at Bald Hill rockhounding site, and what should I know about the terrain?
faq.answer: Dogs are generally welcome on public rockhounding outings in this region, but confirm current site-specific policies with Roxbury town office. The moderate slopes and rocky terrain are manageable for dogs in good condition. Loose rocks and sharp material are present, so keep dogs under control and monitor paw health during extended collecting sessions.

meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and mica at Bald Hill near Roxbury, NH. Easy public rockhounding site with moderate slopes and pegmatite specimens. Plan your visit today.
